raw.reasoning: Since today is Tuesday, 'Tuesday at 3pm' most likely refers to next Tuesday; however, 'this Tuesday' (today at 3pm) is also a reasonable interpretation.
The date 6/15 unambiguously refers to June 15, 2026, which occurs six days after the anchor in the current month.
February 29 does not exist in 2026 (not a leap year), so the next February 29 is clearly 2028-02-29, with minor uncertainty only from the non-existent date in the current year.
5pm most naturally refers to 5 PM today, but could theoretically mean the next occurring 5 PM or a generic 5 PM on an unspecified day.
December 25 is unambiguous; the next occurrence from the anchor (November 26, 2025) is December 25, 2025.
March 4 is unambiguously the 4th day of March; in the context of January 2026, this refers to March 4, 2026.
April 30 is a specific calendar date; since the anchor is in April 2026, 'this' April 30 refers to April 30, 2026.
January 3rd is unambiguous—it clearly refers to the upcoming January 3, 2026, just a few days after the anchor date.
March 4 without a year is a clear reference to the next occurrence of that date; since we are past March 4, 2026 (at June 9, 2026), it resolves to March 4, 2027.
March 4 has already passed in 2026, so the next occurrence (March 4, 2027) is the most natural reading, though a reference to this year's date is also plausible.

Since we're past September 1st (anchor is September 15), 'the 1st' most naturally refers to the next 1st, which is October 1st; however, it could theoretically mean this month's 1st if referencing a date already passed.
From Thursday, 'Tuesday' clearly refers to the next upcoming Tuesday; the 3pm time is explicit, though there's a minor ambiguity about whether a past Tuesday in the same week was intended (unlikely).
The 15th at noon clearly refers to March 15, 2026 (three days ahead) at 12:00, with no ambiguity about which month since we're currently on March 12.
November 5 at noon is the next occurrence of the 5th after the anchor date; month was inferred since 'the 5th' alone lacks explicit month context.
A year ago is an unambiguous reference to exactly one calendar year before the anchor date (2025-03-12).
In two weeks is an unambiguous forward offset from the current moment by exactly 14 days.
A month ago is completely clear: shift the anchor date back by one calendar month using calendar math (same day-of-month: February 12, 2026).
A month ago from the anchor (2026-01-15) unambiguously means 2025-12-15, using calendar month arithmetic which pins the day-of-month.
The phrase 'in 3 business days' is completely clear: advance the anchor by 3 business days (excluding weekends and holidays).
A precise relative time offset: 4 hours before the anchor instant (11:00) is 07:00 on the same day.

Tomorrow is unambiguous (June 10), and adding exactly two weeks yields a specific date (June 24).

The phrase 'the last few months' is clear in intent but 'few' is imprecise—it could reasonably mean 2–4 months, so I chose 3 months as a middle estimate.
The term 'recently' is completely vague and could span anywhere from a few days to several months depending on context; the interpretation as the past week is highly speculative.
Next month is clearly April 2026, but 'early' is inherently vague—typically the first 1–2 weeks, so the first 10 days is a reasonable midpoint interpretation.
Mid-month clearly refers to the current month (June 2026), but the exact boundaries are somewhat flexible—typically understood as the 10th–20th period.
From late December 2025, 'the new year' unambiguously refers to January 1 of the upcoming year (2026).
The weekend clearly refers to Saturday and Sunday of this week (June 13–14), though the previous weekend is a less common but plausible alternative interpretation.
"Soon" is inherently vague without context about what event or action is being referenced; the intended span could range from hours to weeks.
"This afternoon" unambiguously refers to the afternoon hours (noon to 6 PM) of today, 2026-06-09.
End of year clearly refers to the end of the current calendar year (December 31, 2025), though it could mean either a range to that date or just the specific final day.
Next Friday is completely unambiguous from a Tuesday anchor—it refers to the Friday coming up in the same week (June 12, 2026).
"Around the holidays" refers to the general holiday season spanning roughly late November through early January, but lacks specificity about which holidays are primary and exact boundaries.
One calendar month prior (Feb 12) is the clear intent; 'about' introduces minor fuzziness but the phrasing maps straightforwardly to calendar-month arithmetic.
In late November, 'the long weekend' almost certainly refers to Thanksgiving (Thursday, November 27, 2025), and the phrase requests the period before it begins.
The upcoming summer 2026 (June through August) is the clear referent from the March 2026 anchor, though the exact calendar boundaries of 'summer' have minor seasonal ambiguity.
